text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic subtotal colectomy with a right hemicolectomy, locating the ileocecal junction and grasping the peritoneum to create tension on the ileocolic pedicle. This tension aids in opening the peritoneal plane, which is gently dissected to approach the duodenum in a medial to lateral direction.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the ileocolic pedicle and carefully grasps the superior layer of the peritoneum to safely retract the duodenum. The dissection proceeds medially above the duodenum, using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques to avoid tearing small veins near the head of the pancreas. After securing the duodenum, the dissection shifts laterally to continue the right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures, aiming to identify the fascia of Toldt. The dissection is challenging due to edematous tissue and ascites, visible in the surgical field. A small swab or gauze is used to aid in the dissection. After completing the medial to lateral dissection and demonstrating the fascia of Toldt, attention is shifted to the lateral dissection of congenital adhesions.

The surgeon uses strong traction and counter traction to dissect the hepatic flexure from the inferior colic compartment, carefully lifting the medial to lateral part to separate the hepatic flexure while being mindful of the underlying duodenum.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic omentum in the supracolic compartment, visibly stretching between the stomach and transverse colon. Care is taken to avoid damaging the stomach's blood supply by staying close to the colonic side. The dissection progresses through the gastrocolic omentum, opening the lesser sac from the hepatic flexure to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon demonstrates the stomach and transverse colon, with multiple benign lymph nodes visible. Careful lifting of the transverse colon is performed to avoid damaging the underlying duodenum and pancreas, especially in a thin patient.

The surgeon diverts attention to the left colon, positioning the patient in a steep head-down tilt with the table tilted left side up to clear the pelvis of the small bowel. The dissection proceeds inward at the level of the splenic flexure, moving from left to right towards the previously dissected transverse mesocolon. The greater omentum is densely adhered to the spleen, requiring meticulous dissection to preserve the splenic blood supply.

During the subtotal colectomy for benign disease, the surgeon performs a left-sided dissection from lateral to medial, avoiding proximity to the origin of blood vessels and instead dissecting close to the colon to minimize risk to structures like the ureter.

The surgeon performs dissection near the sacral promontory and divides the lower sigmoid and upper rectum using a transfer stapler. Post-operative abdominal collections are noted, which were drained radiologically.
